item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Odd, I had found it in this source as well, which states to use it's contents "solely for personal and non-commercial purposes" according to its Terms of Use. However, as the original image has been confirmed to be public domain, I've put it back on. Thank you, C-Dives.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
It may sound strange, but I found its image source on Wikimedia Commons some months ago.
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oculus-Rift-CV1-Headset-Front.jpg
Image is released into the public domain by its author, so I don't think this is non-compliant with SCP wiki image license.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article was non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to the presence of "NC" in the image's license. Thus, it has been removed.

EDIT: The image has been put back up after it was determined to be cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be nice if the article justified the insane amount of memories users are going to acquire. Perhaps they don't retain everything, or it jumps ahead to significant events (it is a simulation). As it stands now, the researcher hasn't been at work for 89 lifetimes. That's going to cause some issues.

Still a +1, because besides those bits I love the concept and execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know about prior versions, but the current version lasts until the subject's death in the simulation, including someone who became an art expert and met many anartists.

I assume everyone has watched Groundhogs Day, but that is a great showing how something like that could be a torture but that one (might) come out the other side enlightened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2 thoughts. well, more than 2 but 2 I want to post here.

1- I wonder if the sociopath stayed in by choice or by never learning and never seeing the "i understand" option. On first read I took it to be that he was incapable of understanding so was trapped in what would eventually become a torture. On second read though, assume that he saw "I understand"….why would he clikc that? why not do it again and again as king of his own universe, unable to kill the people he killed in real life, but able to do literally anything else? (Maybe someone in that mindset would never understand, but would be happy about it rather than feeling trapped)

2-Should the last one be "estimated" for the number of iterations? The subject could not report it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only the 24 hour loop, I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Likewise, I thought this was amazing during the drafting stage, and I am very glad to see it up here.

Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it has trouble with people whose mental state is not normal when they killed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I don't get is the last test…the car accident one….is that because she was sort of responsible and the headset didn't know which way to go with it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shout-out and many thanks to Zyn for taking care of this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, quick note, I noticed that you didn't localize the image properly. Would you like help with that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I am a bit confused. Does the subject live out their entire life every instance? If so, I feel like that would drive me mad more then provide me with mental and spiritual healing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely something ambitious, and it does the emotional angle very well. I said this during the draft stage, and I'll say it again—I'm very pleased to see an atypical take on the Oculus Rift VR anomalous item that isn't just "wow VR is super cool!".

Solid +1 from me here; excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid, yet intricate idea. The execution was definitely spot-on— I especially loved the testing log! It's one of the more emotional articles I've read in a while.

I do have one small thing to mention, though. I'm aware by the testing log that these instances of the game, while they could last up to several years in the simulation, only last a fraction of a second in real life. Shouldn't that property also be mentioned somewhere in the description?

Otherwise, nice! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe this was edited since last time I read the article, and after a full reread, I don't think I found any mistakes. The only suggestion I can really think of is the addition of how the Foundation came into possession of -4908. Anyways, congratz on this SCP, I think it's going to be a great permanent addition!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its better than a lot of the mediocre skips being submitted rn, so +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey 51 47,

Thanks so much for the feedback! Mind pointing me towards the formatting errors you've come across? Would love to get those fixed up ASAP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey plaguebearer —

Thanks for that! Sorry, I thought I had highlighted everything in the sandbox page but I guess I missed that kind of important part. Oops!

Also, mind elaborating on the formatting errors? Would love to fix those up ASAP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: added a rating module, also this is cool aside from the formatting errors

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Neat article, but I'm holding my vote for now. A few formatting errors, things like that, but otherwise pretty cool.

EDIT: Actually I'll just +1 this for the overall quality.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks go out to GentleGifts, Uncannyon, and Zyn for all taking the time to look at this in its various draft forms!

Dedicated to the memory of Jim, the man who would've been my father-in-law.

Not necessarily related to the SCP itself, but on the subject of grief… Some wise words from one of the most beautiful Reddit comments I've yet to come across:

Text of Reddit comment, for those who'd rather not click through
Close

"Alright, here goes. I'm old. What that means is that I've survived (so far) and a lot of people I've known and loved did not. I've lost friends, best friends, acquaintances, co-workers, grandparents, mom, relatives, teachers, mentors, students, neighbors, and a host of other folks. I have no children, and I can't imagine the pain it must be to lose a child. But here's my two cents.

I wish I could say you get used to people dying. I never did. I don't want to. It tears a hole through me whenever somebody I love dies, no matter the circumstances. But I don't want it to "not matter". I don't want it to be something that just passes. My scars are a testament to the love and the relationship that I had for and with that person. And if the scar is deep, so was the love. So be it. Scars are a testament to life. Scars are a testament that I can love deeply and live deeply and be cut, or even gouged, and that I can heal and continue to live and continue to love. And the scar tissue is stronger than the original flesh ever was. Scars are a testament to life. Scars are only ugly to people who can't see.

As for grief, you'll find it comes in waves. When the ship is first wrecked, you're drowning, with wreckage all around you. Everything floating around you reminds you of the beauty and the magnificence of the ship that was, and is no more. And all you can do is float. You find some piece of the wreckage and you hang on for a while. Maybe it's some physical thing. Maybe it's a happy memory or a photograph. Maybe it's a person who is also floating. For a while, all you can do is float. Stay alive.

In the beginning, the waves are 100 feet tall and crash over you without mercy. They come 10 seconds apart and don't even give you time to catch your breath. All you can do is hang on and float. After a while, maybe weeks, maybe months, you'll find the waves are still 100 feet tall, but they come further apart. When they come, they still crash all over you and wipe you out. But in between, you can breathe, you can function. You never know what's going to trigger the grief. It might be a song, a picture, a street intersection, the smell of a cup of coffee. It can be just about anything…and the wave comes crashing. But in between waves, there is life.

Somewhere down the line, and it's different for everybody, you find that the waves are only 80 feet tall. Or 50 feet tall. And while they still come, they come further apart. You can see them coming. An anniversary, a birthday, or Christmas, or landing at O'Hare. You can see it coming, for the most part, and prepare yourself. And when it washes over you, you know that somehow you will, again, come out the other side. Soaking wet, sputtering, still hanging on to some tiny piece of the wreckage, but you'll come out.

Take it from an old guy. The waves never stop coming, and somehow you don't really want them to. But you learn that you'll survive them. And other waves will come. And you'll survive them too. If you're lucky, you'll have lots of scars from lots of loves. And lots of shipwrecks."
